records.ety: [Akin to D. ratelen, G. rasseln, AS. hrætele a rattle, in hrætelwyrt rattlewort; cf. Gr. kradai`nein to swing, wave. Cf. Rail a bird.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Rattled (-t'ld); p. pr. & vb. n. Rattling (-tlng).]

defs: To make a quick succession of sharp, inharmonious noises, as by the collision of hard and not very sonorous bodies shaken together; to clatter.
quotes: And the rude hail in rattling tempest forms.
'T was but the wind, Or the car rattling o'er the stony street.

defs: To drive or ride briskly, so as to make a clattering; as, we rattled along for a couple of miles.
To make a clatter with the voice; to talk rapidly and idly; to clatter; -- with on or away; as, she rattled on for an hour.
To cause to make a rattling or clattering sound; as, to rattle a chain.
To assail, annoy, or stun with a rattling noise.
quotes: Sound but another [drum], and another shall As loud as thine rattle the welkin's ear.

defs: Hence, to disconcert; to confuse; as, to rattle one's judgment; to rattle a player in a game.
A rapid succession of sharp, clattering sounds; as, the rattle of a drum.

quotes: All this ado about the golden age is but an empty rattle and frivolous conceit.

defs: An instrument with which a rattling sound is made; especially, a child's toy that rattles when shaken.
quotes: The rattles of Isis and the cymbals of Brasilea nearly enough resemble each other.
Pleased with a rattle, tickled with a straw.

defs: A noisy, senseless talker; a jabberer.
quotes: It may seem strange that a man who wrote with so much perspicuity, vivacity, and grace, should have been, whenever he took a part in conversation, an empty, noisy, blundering rattle.

defs: Any organ of an animal having a structure adapted to produce a rattling sound.
notes: ☞ The rattle of a rattlesnake is composed of the hardened terminal scales, loosened in succession, but not cast off, and so modified in form as to make a series of loose, hollow joints.

defs: The noise in the throat produced by the air in passing through mucus which the lungs are unable to expel; -- chiefly observable at the approach of death, when it is called the death rattle. See Rle.
